mission.title: Generalization Validation (L2) — four-day work week productivity
mission.decision_supported: Whether NH should mention the four-day work week as an evidence-supported practice in an internal operations note.
research_question: Does adopting a four-day work week (100-80-100 model) maintain or improve organizational productivity, and how well does the evidence generalize?
mission.scope: Organized researcher-evaluated four-day-week pilots ~2015-2024; office/knowledge-work firms; public reports.
mission.out_of_scope: Mandated whole-economy policy; sectors not covered by the pilots (heavy manufacturing, most healthcare); legal/regulatory analysis.
mission.reviewer: Founder (author-reviewer; L2 — independent reviewer preferred, not required, per NH-ROP §18)

claims.statement: Organized, researcher-evaluated four-day-week (100-80-100) trials have been conducted, notably a 61-company UK pilot in 2022.
claims.confidence_rationale: The UK 2022 pilot is documented across multiple secondary summaries with named academic evaluators; an Iceland public-sector trial is also reported (less independently verified here).
claims.overturn_conditions: Evidence that the reported pilots did not occur or were not researcher-evaluated.
claims.origin: UK 4 Day Week pilot 2022; Iceland public-sector trials.

claims.statement: In the UK 2022 pilot, about 92% of participating companies (56/61) continued the four-day week immediately after, and about 89% (54/61) one year later.
claims.scope: The 61 participating firms of the UK 2022 pilot.
claims.confidence_rationale: Continuation figures are consistently reported by the pilot organizers and follow-up; population is the self-selected pilot firms, not employers generally.
claims.overturn_conditions: A corrected count materially changing the continuation rate.
claims.origin: UK 4 Day Week pilot results + one-year follow-up.

claims.statement: Adopting a four-day week causes maintained or improved organizational productivity.
claims.confidence_rationale: Supporting productivity/revenue signals are largely self-reported and come from self-selected firms; no randomized design with objective output metrics isolates a causal effect.
claims.overturn_conditions: A controlled study with objective output measures showing a clear causal effect (either direction).

claims.statement: Participants in the UK pilot reported large wellbeing gains (e.g., ~71% reduced burnout, ~39% less stressed).
claims.confidence_rationale: Consistently reported wellbeing figures; establishes reported participant experience, not a generalizable causal effect.
claims.overturn_conditions: Evidence the wellbeing figures were misreported or reversed after the pilot.
claims.origin: UK 4 Day Week pilot wellbeing survey data.

claims.statement: The four-day week generalizes to all sectors and organization types.
claims.confidence_rationale: It worked for the participating office/knowledge-work firms, but pilots skewed to self-selected white-collar organizations; shift, manufacturing, and most healthcare settings are under-represented.
claims.overturn_conditions: Broad multi-sector evidence (incl. shift/manufacturing/healthcare) of comparable results.
claims.origin: Pilot success in tested firms vs sector-skew limitation.

claims.statement: Rigorous randomized controlled evidence with objective output metrics isolating the four-day week's causal productivity effect is limited.
claims.scope: Published four-day-week productivity evidence to date.
claims.confidence_rationale: Within the reviewed public evidence, pilots are observational, volunteer-based, and rely on self-reported output; no qualifying RCT with objective metrics was identified. This does not prove none exists.
claims.overturn_conditions: A randomized controlled four-day-week study with objective output metrics.
claims.origin: Methodological critique of the pilot evidence base.

claims.statement: Adopting a four-day week reduces a firm's total energy use / carbon footprint.
claims.scope: Firm-level energy use / carbon footprint under a four-day week.
claims.confidence_rationale: No evidence measuring the energy/carbon effect of a four-day week was found in the reviewed public scope; the claim is neither supported nor disproved.
claims.overturn_conditions: A study measuring firm-level energy use / carbon under a four-day week.
claims.origin: Plausible sustainability hypothesis; not evidenced in the reviewed scope.

evidence.exact_finding: A 61-company, ~2,900-worker UK four-day-week pilot ran Jun-Dec 2022, evaluated by Boston College, Cambridge and Autonomy.
evidence.extraction_method: web search of pilot results summaries
evidence.location: UK 4 Day Week pilot results report (Feb 2023)
evidence.limitations: reached via secondary summaries; primary report not fetched; advocacy-run pilot

evidence.exact_finding: Iceland ran large public-sector four-day/reduced-hours trials (2015-2019) reported as broadly successful.
evidence.extraction_method: prior knowledge; not re-fetched this session
evidence.limitations: not verified this session; included cautiously as a second instance

evidence.exact_finding: 56 of 61 firms (92%) continued immediately; 54 of 61 (89%) were still operating the policy one year on.
evidence.location: UK pilot results + 'Making it stick' one-year follow-up (2024)

evidence.exact_finding: Company revenue was reported as broadly stable during the pilot.
evidence.limitations: self-reported; no objective output metric; self-selected firms

evidence.exact_finding: Volunteer-firm selection bias plus self-reported productivity/revenue undermine causal inference from the pilots.
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: self-selected firms + self-reported metrics != causal proof
evidence.location: critique of volunteer-based pilots (partly acknowledged in the pilot reports)
evidence.limitations: a critique of design, not a counter-measurement

evidence.exact_finding: 71% of employees reported reduced burnout and 39% reported less stress at the end of the UK pilot.
The policy worked across the participating firms (high continuation, stable revenue, better wellbeing).
Pilot participants skewed to self-selected office/knowledge-work firms; shift, manufacturing and most healthcare are under-represented.
No randomized controlled four-day-week study with objective output metrics was identified in the reviewed public evidence.
evidence.quote_or_paraphrase: no qualifying RCT with objective output identified
evidence.limitations: absence in reviewed scope; not proof of non-existence

sources.title: The results are in: the UK's four-day week pilot (+ 'Making it stick' one-year follow-up)
sources.author_or_org: Autonomy / 4 Day Week Global / Boston College / University of Cambridge
sources.commercial_interest: advocacy: 4 Day Week Global / Autonomy promote the policy
sources.reliability_notes: academic co-evaluators (Boston College/Cambridge) lend independence, but advocacy-run pilot and largely self-reported productivity/revenue

sources.title: Iceland public-sector reduced-hours / four-day trials (2015-2019)
sources.reliability_notes: included cautiously as a second trial instance; not verified this session

sources.title: Methodological critique of volunteer-based four-day-week pilots (selection bias; self-reported output; sector skew)
sources.url_or_internal_location: critique synthesized from pilot-design caveats and public commentary
sources.reliability_notes: limitations are partly acknowledged within the pilot reports themselves (self-selection; self-reported revenue)

assumptions.statement: Results from self-selected volunteer-firm pilots generalize to mandated adoption and to non-office sectors.
assumptions.why_needed: to move from pilot outcomes to a general recommendation.
assumptions.evidence_status: unsupported by the reviewed evidence (selection bias)

unknowns.exact_open_question: What is the four-day week's effect on objective (non-self-reported) productivity output, especially in non-office sectors?
unknowns.why_it_matters: the causal claim (C3) and universality (C5) both hinge on objective, cross-sector output.
search_attempts: public search of four-day-week pilot results (2026-07-18)
unknowns.missing_evidence: randomized controlled study with objective output metrics across sectors
unknowns.recommended_next_action: seek or commission an objective-output multi-sector study

contradictions.nature_of_conflict: Self-reported pilot productivity/continuation signals vs the limited rigorous causal evidence base.
contradictions.scope_differences: self-selected office firms vs general economy
contradictions.methodological_differences: observational volunteer pilots vs randomized controlled measurement
contradictions.impact_on_verdict: keeps C3 at Inconclusive; supports C5 scope-qualification and C6.

verdicts.confidence_rationale: Multiple secondary summaries + named academic evaluators; second instance less verified.
verdicts.overturn_conditions: pilots shown not to have occurred/been evaluated.

verdicts.confidence_rationale: Self-reported signals from self-selected firms; no objective causal isolation.
verdicts.evidence_summary: self-reported stable revenue (Supports) vs selection-bias critique (Contradicts).

verdicts.confidence_rationale: Consistently reported wellbeing; reported experience, not causal generalization.
Works for tested white-collar firms; sector skew limits universality.
verdicts.evidence_summary: worked for participants (Supports) vs sector skew (Limits).

verdicts.confidence_rationale: No qualifying RCT with objective output found in reviewed scope; not proof of non-existence.
No energy/carbon measurement located in the reviewed scope; neither supported nor disproved.
verdicts.scope_of_validity: firm energy/carbon under a four-day week.
verdicts.overturn_conditions: a study measuring the energy/carbon effect.
